name: Guptas, Delhi Sultans, Mughals, and Marathas

rauias.com, worldhistory.org, anantamias.com, exampivot.com, civilserviceaspirants.in — Gupta dynasty founders/titles (Sri Gupta, Chandragupta I, Samudragupta's Allahabad Pillar/Napoleon-of-India title, Chandragupta II Vikramaditya/Mehrauli Iron Pillar/Fa-Hien), Mughal-era trivia (Shah Jahan 'Prince of Builders', Humayun's name meaning and Battle of Kanauj/Bilgram loss to Sher Khan)

researchNotes: Strong real evidence across all but the Maratha portion of this four-dynasty clause (4 confirmed hits spanning Gupta, Delhi Sultanate, and Mughal eras across 2024/2026 papers) — Maratha-specific facts came from web research only, clearly tagged. Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned the OCR bank with dynasty/ruler-name keywords (not just 'gupta'/'sultan', which drowned in false positives from 'industries'/'Sultan of Oman'-style current-affairs hits) and found FIVE more genuine, image-verified past-paper hits spanning Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi, Alauddin Khilji's conquests and architecture, a Thanjavur-Maratha art fact, and the Treaty of Bassein — plus caught and corrected nothing wrong here, but did independently verify each answer key checkmark against the actual page image rather than trusting OCR alone. Also closed a real gap: the original pass had zero facts about the Gupta dynasty's actual rulers/titles (only a coin-metal fact), despite 'Guptas' being the first-named dynasty in this syllabus clause — added founder Sri Gupta, Chandragupta I's title, Samudragupta's Allahabad Pillar/Napoleon-of-India epithet, and Chandragupta II's Vikramaditya title/Mehrauli Pillar/Fa-Hien visit, all standard NCERT-level facts cross-checked across multiple reference sites. A Bahmani-Sultanate-style 'peace-loving vs cruel ruler' epithet pair found on one coaching aggregator for two Bahmani sultans could NOT be independently confirmed against reliable sources (Wikipedia/ClearIAS actually complicate the 'peace-loving' framing) and was deliberately left out rather than risk shipping a shaky claim — that fact lives in the Vijayanagar/Bahmani topic's territory anyway, not this one.

reading.intro: Second topic of Unit III, spanning four major medieval/early-modern dynasties in one syllabus clause. Real exam evidence favors specific administrative/terminology facts (coin metals, tax names, official titles) over broad narrative history.

qa.question: Real exam: What metal did the Gupta emperors issue coins in, before Chandragupta-II?
qa.answer: Gold only — the Gupta emperors before Chandragupta-II issued only gold coins (Chandragupta-II later expanded to silver coinage too).

qa.question: Real exam: What does 'Haque-i-Sharb' (or 'Hasil-i-Sharb') mean, in the Delhi Sultanate's revenue system?
qa.answer: Irrigation Tax (not land tax, commercial tax, or toll tax).

qa.question: Real exam: How is the modern term 'Tahsildar' connected to Mughal-era terminology?
qa.answer: The word 'Tahsildar' derives from the Mughal word 'tahsil', meaning a 'revenue-generating area'. (A related real-exam fact: since the Tahsil was renamed 'Taluk', the Tahsildars are now correspondingly called 'Taluk Officers' — both statements were marked true, but the question noted the second does NOT serve as the explanation for the first.)

qa.question: Real exam: Who is the only woman historian known to have written a historical account of the Mughal period?
qa.answer: Gulbadan Begum (Humayun's half-sister), author of the Humayun-nama — not Zeenat-un-Nisa Begum, Jahanara Begum, or Zeb-un-Nissa Begum (the other options in that real question).

qa.question: When and where was Shivaji crowned, and what concept did his coronation assert?
qa.answer: 6 June 1674, at Raigad Fort. His coronation (Rajyabhishek) asserted 'Hindavi Swarajya' (self-rule) — an independent state not owing allegiance to the Mughal empire.

qa.question: What was the 'Ashta Pradhan', and what were its key positions?
qa.answer: Shivaji's council of eight ministers. Key positions: Peshwa (Prime Minister, who also supervised civil and military affairs), Amatya (Finance Minister), Senapati (Commander-in-Chief), and Nyayadhish (Chief Justice).

qa.question: What were Chauth and Sardeshmukhi, the Maratha revenue systems?
qa.answer: Chauth: one-fourth of land revenue paid BY OTHER territories to the Marathas to avoid being raided. Sardeshmukhi: an additional 10% levy on lands where the Marathas claimed hereditary rights.

qa.question: What was 'Ganimi Kava', and why was it militarily significant?
qa.answer: Shivaji's guerrilla warfare tactics — using the Sahyadri mountain terrain to let smaller Maratha forces defeat much larger conventional Mughal armies.

qa.question: Who founded the Gupta dynasty, and which later Gupta ruler was the first to issue gold coins and take the title 'Maharajadhiraja'?
qa.answer: Sri Gupta founded the dynasty (as a minor local ruler). Chandragupta I (Sri Gupta's grandson) was the first Gupta ruler to appear on gold coins and the first to adopt the imperial title 'Maharajadhiraja' (king of great kings), marking the dynasty's rise to major power.

qa.question: Who was Samudragupta, and what evidence/epithets describe his reign?
qa.answer: Samudragupta (son of Chandragupta I) is often called the 'Napoleon of India' for his extensive military conquests, recorded in the Allahabad Pillar (Prayaga Prashasti) inscription composed by his court poet Harisena. He was also a poet and musician himself, reflected in his title 'Kaviraja' (king of poets).

qa.question: Who was Chandragupta II, what title did he take, and which famous visitor/monument are linked to his reign?
qa.answer: Chandragupta II (Samudragupta's son) took the title 'Vikramaditya' after conquering the Western Kshatrapas (Shaka rulers of Gujarat/Malwa), gaining access to western seaports. The Mehrauli Iron Pillar (Delhi) — remarkably rust-resistant after 1,600+ years — commemorates his achievements. The Chinese Buddhist pilgrim Fa-Hien visited India during his reign and recorded a peaceful, prosperous kingdom.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q1 page 93 Q157): Which statement about Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi is correct?
qa.answer: It aimed 'to establish a national religion which would be acceptable to both the Muslims and the Hindus' — NOT that it had a large following (it actually had very few followers), NOT that it was a formal 'state religion', and NOT that it aimed at territorial expansion.

qa.question: Real exam (2022_q1 page 53 Q174): Arrange Alauddin Khilji's territorial conquests in chronological order — Ranthambor, Malwa, Chittor, Gujarat.
qa.answer: Gujarat (1299) → Ranthambor (1301) → Chittor (1303) → Malwa (1305) — the exam's marked answer was 'IV, I, III, II' in that original listing order.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q1 page 70 Q130): Which historians' statements about Khilji-era architecture are TRUE — K.S. Lal on a new Hindu-Muslim architectural style, Marshall on Hauz-i-Khas, or Percy Brown on Alauddin's mosque gateway?
qa.answer: (i) and (iii) only are true: K.S. Lal's observation about a new Hindu-Muslim architectural era, and Percy Brown's remark that Alauddin Khilji's mosque gateway occupies a key position in Indian Islamic architecture's evolution. The Hauz-i-Khas praise is misattributed to Marshall in that option (making it false as stated).

qa.question: Real exam (2017_q page 21 Q80): A painting of which dynasty's king welcomes visitors to the Durbar Hall in Thanjavur?
qa.answer: A Maratha king — reflecting the Maratha rule of Thanjavur (Thanjavur Marathas, a separate offshoot dynasty from Shivaji's line) after they took over from the Nayaks, with their palace/Durbar Hall complex still standing in Thanjavur today.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q page 81 Q85): Which statements about the Treaty of Bassein (1802) are true?
qa.answer: All three: (i) it was signed between Peshwa Baji Rao II and the British; (ii) the Peshwa agreed to permanently cede territories yielding ₹26 lakh income to the East India Company; (iii) the Peshwa agreed to surrender the city of Surat to the Company. This treaty effectively made the Peshwa (and by extension, much of the Maratha confederacy) a British protectorate, triggering the Second Anglo-Maratha War.

questions.prompt: The Gupta emperors before Chandragupta-II issued coins in which metal only?
In the Delhi Sultanate's revenue system, 'Haque-i-Sharb' means:
The modern term 'Tahsildar' derives from the Mughal word 'tahsil', meaning:
Who is the only woman historian known to have written a historical account of the Mughal period?
questions.explanation: Gulbadan Begum, author of the Humayun-nama.

questions.prompt: Shivaji's coronation in 1674 took place at:
In Shivaji's Ashta Pradhan, who held the position of Finance Minister?
'Chauth', the Maratha tax, was equal to what fraction of a territory's land revenue?
Shivaji's guerrilla warfare tactics, exploiting the Sahyadri terrain, were known as:
Real exam (2025_q1 Q157): Which statement about Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi is correct?
options: It had a large number of followers
It was a formal state religion
It aimed to establish a national religion acceptable to both Muslims and Hindus
It aimed to expand territory and control independent states
questions.explanation: It aimed to establish a national religion acceptable to both communities — it actually had very few followers and was never a formal state religion.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2022_q1 Q174): In which chronological order did Alauddin Khilji conquer Ranthambor, Malwa, Chittor, and Gujarat?
questions.trick: Remember Khilji's expansion moved roughly west-to-east-and-back over 6 years: Gujarat first, then the Rajasthan forts (Ranthambor, Chittor), then Malwa last.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q1 Q130): Whose remark correctly describes Alauddin Khilji's mosque gateway as occupying 'a key position in the evolution of Islamic architecture in India'?
questions.explanation: Percy Brown (the Marshall/Hauz-i-Khas pairing in the same question was the false statement).

questions.prompt: Real exam (2017_q Q80): The painting of a king of which dynasty welcomes visitors to the Durbar Hall in Thanjavur?
questions.explanation: Maratha — reflecting the Thanjavur Marathas' rule after the Nayaks.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q Q85): The Treaty of Bassein (1802) was signed between the British and which Peshwa?
questions.explanation: Peshwa Baji Rao II, who also ceded territory worth ₹26 lakh income and surrendered Surat to the British.

questions.prompt: Who founded the Gupta dynasty?
Which Gupta ruler was the first to issue gold coins and adopt the title 'Maharajadhiraja'?
Samudragupta, often called the 'Napoleon of India', had his military conquests recorded in which inscription?
questions.explanation: The Allahabad Pillar inscription, composed by his court poet Harisena.

questions.prompt: Chandragupta II adopted the title 'Vikramaditya' after defeating which rulers, gaining access to western seaports?
questions.explanation: The Western Kshatrapas (Shaka rulers of Gujarat and Malwa).

questions.prompt: Which Chinese Buddhist pilgrim visited India during Chandragupta II's reign and recorded a peaceful, prosperous kingdom?
Which Mughal emperor was called the 'Prince of Builders' for his architectural projects (like the Taj Mahal)?
Humayun, whose name ironically means 'Fortune', is often called the Mughal Empire's most unfortunate ruler after losing the 1540 Battle of Kanauj (also called the Battle of Bilgram) to whom?
questions.explanation: Sher Khan (Sher Shah Suri), which forced Humayun into 15 years of exile before he recovered the throne.
